summary: McKenzie describes porting Bingo Card Creator to Mac OS X, motivated by a dedicated customer who bought the software before the Mac version existed. Rather than learning the Mac toolchain himself, he outsourced the native wrapper creation on RentACoder for $25. The Java codebase required zero lines of code changes for the port. He notes that Mac users represent about 2% of his site traffic but feels committed to the platform given he already has a paying customer. The post also briefly covers progress on version 1.04 with new features and word lists.
key_points: A single dedicated customer who pre-purchased the Mac version created enough commitment to justify the port
The entire Mac port required zero code changes thanks to Java's cross-platform nature, only a native wrapper
Outsourcing the native wrapper creation via RentACoder cost just $25, demonstrating extreme cost efficiency for a micro-ISV
Mac market share among his audience was only ~2%, making the business case marginal but the effort trivial enough to justify
money_quotes: She asked me whether I would have the Mac version done soon and I told her "Well, I certainly hope so". And then she went ahead and bought it, sight unseen.
I tried to refund her money because I can't take money for a product which doesn't officially exist yet but she would have none of it.
She sends me updates on how her son is loving reading and math nowadays.
I offered the project up at $25 to port Bingo Card Creator (where "port" means "spend five minutes creating a native wrapper").
Number of lines of codes changed for the Mac version? Erm, zero. I like being a Java Success Story (TM).
Is there a market for Bingo Card Creator on the Mac? Well, even if there wasn't, I've got one customer who bought it already and so I'm rather committed.
key_insight: Even tiny markets are worth serving when the marginal cost of entry is near zero, and a single passionate customer can justify an entire platform port.
